title: What Is Section 106 of the NHPA? A Complete Guide
content:
Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) is one of the most important federal compliance requirements for any project involving federal funding, permits, or licenses. Whether you're a federal agency, state DOT, or private developer working on a federally assisted project, understanding Section 106 is essential to staying compliant and avoiding costly project delays.

Quick Answer: Section 106 of the NHPA requires federal agencies to consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties before approving or funding projects. The process involves identifying historic properties, assessing adverse effects, and consulting with stakeholders to resolve those effects—typically completed in 30–90 days for straightforward projects.

The Section 106 review process is not optional—it applies to any undertaking with a federal nexus, including highway projects, utility infrastructure, land development on federal lands, and projects receiving federal grants. Navigating this process correctly from the start protects project timelines and ensures culturally significant places are properly considered.

What Does Section 106 of the NHPA Actually Require?

Section 106 requires federal agencies to take into account the effects of their undertakings on properties listed in or eligible for the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP). The law does not mandate preservation—it mandates a good-faith consultation process before a project moves forward.

The consulting parties include the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO), Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs), local governments, and the public. Together, these parties work through a structured review to identify historic properties within the Area of Potential Effect (APE) and determine whether the project will cause an adverse effect.

If adverse effects are found, the agency must negotiate a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or Programmatic Agreement (PA) that outlines steps to mitigate those effects. This can include archaeological data recovery, architectural documentation, or project redesign. The Advisory Council on Historic Preservation (ACHP) oversees the process and may participate in complex or high-profile cases.

How Does the Section 106 Process Work Step by Step?

The Section 106 process follows four key steps defined in 36 CFR Part 800, the implementing regulations of the NHPA. Understanding each step helps project teams plan timelines and budgets accurately.

Step 1 – Initiate the Process: The lead federal agency identifies the undertaking, determines the APE, and begins consultation with SHPOs, THPOs, and other interested parties. Early coordination is critical here—delays in this step cascade through the entire review.

Step 2 – Identify Historic Properties: An archaeological survey or architectural survey is conducted within the APE to identify any properties that are listed in or eligible for the NRHP. This may include Phase I archaeological surveys, historic structure assessments, and background research.

Step 3 – Assess Adverse Effects: Once historic properties are identified, the agency applies the Criteria of Adverse Effect (36 CFR 800.5) to determine whether the project will diminish the integrity or significance of those properties. Adverse effects include physical destruction, change of character, introduction of visual or noise intrusions, and neglect.

Step 4 – Resolve Adverse Effects: If an adverse effect is found, the agency consults with the SHPO/THPO and other parties to develop an MOA or PA that details mitigation measures. Once signed by all parties, the agency may proceed with the undertaking in compliance with the agreement's terms.

Who Is Responsible for Section 106 Compliance?

The federal agency with jurisdiction over the undertaking bears primary responsibility for Section 106 compliance. However, in practice, this obligation is commonly delegated to project applicants—such as developers, transportation agencies, or utilities—who must fund and manage the required surveys and consultations.

Under 36 CFR 800.2(c)(4), federal agencies may authorize applicants to initiate consultation on their behalf. This means private developers receiving federal permits (such as Clean Water Act Section 404 permits from the Army Corps of Engineers) often carry significant responsibility for driving the Section 106 process forward.

Cultural resource management (CRM) firms like TVAR play a key role here. A qualified CRM firm manages the archaeological and historic preservation surveys, prepares the necessary reports, coordinates with the SHPO, and helps applicants and agencies navigate the consultation process efficiently. Selecting an experienced firm early reduces risk and accelerates timelines.

What Triggers a Section 106 Review?

A Section 106 review is triggered any time there is a "federal undertaking"—defined broadly as any project, activity, or program funded in whole or in part by a federal agency, or requiring a federal permit, license, or approval. The federal nexus, not project size or type, is the determining factor.

Common triggers include: highway and transportation projects funded by the Federal Highway Administration (FHWA); utility projects requiring Army Corps permits; housing and community development projects funded by HUD; energy infrastructure on federal lands or requiring FERC permits; and telecommunications projects involving FCC licenses.

Even relatively small projects can trigger Section 106 if they have a federal connection. For example, a private developer who needs a Section 404 wetland permit for a commercial development must comply with Section 106 requirements tied to that Army Corps authorization. Understanding your federal nexus early is the first step toward compliance.

How Long Does Section 106 Take and What Does It Cost?

Timeline and cost vary depending on project complexity, the density of historic properties in the APE, and the responsiveness of consulting parties. Simple undertakings with no historic properties may be resolved in 30–60 days. Projects in archaeologically sensitive areas or involving significant historic structures can take six months to several years.

Standard SHPO review timelines run 30 days for each formal consultation step, meaning a straightforward "no historic properties affected" finding might conclude in 60–90 days. More complex findings requiring an MOA add additional consultation and negotiation time. Programmatic Agreements for large linear projects or multi-year programs can take 12–18 months to develop.

Cost is similarly variable. A Phase I archaeological survey for a small commercial site may run $5,000–$15,000. Data recovery excavations (Phase III) for significant sites can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars. Early investment in GIS and remote sensing analysis helps project teams predict where historic properties are likely to occur, focusing survey effort and controlling costs. Learn more about survey phases in our guide to archaeological survey services.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Section 106 and Section 110 of the NHPA?

Section 106 is a project-specific review process requiring federal agencies to consider effects on historic properties before approving undertakings. Section 110 establishes affirmative responsibilities for federal agencies to identify, protect, and nominate historic properties under their ownership or control. Both sections apply to federal agencies but serve different purposes.

Does Section 106 apply to private projects with no federal funding?

Section 106 only applies when there is a federal nexus—federal funding, a federal permit, or federal land. A purely private project with no federal involvement is not subject to Section 106. However, many private projects require federal permits (such as Army Corps Section 404 permits), which create the necessary federal nexus and trigger the review.

What happens if a federal agency skips the Section 106 process?

Failure to comply with Section 106 can result in project injunctions, litigation, and required mitigation after the fact—often at much greater cost than proactive compliance. Courts have halted construction projects due to Section 106 violations. Federal agencies and applicants that skip the process also risk damaging relationships with SHPOs, Tribes, and the ACHP.

What is a Programmatic Agreement under Section 106?

A Programmatic Agreement (PA) is a negotiated document used when the effects of an undertaking are complex, multi-phased, or cannot be fully assessed upfront. PAs establish a framework for how historic properties will be identified and treated over the life of a project. They are common for large infrastructure programs, land management plans, and multi-year undertakings.

How do Tribal Historic Preservation Offices (THPOs) fit into Section 106?

THPOs are federally recognized Tribal governments that have assumed SHPO responsibilities for their tribal lands. They are consulting parties under Section 106 for undertakings that may affect historic properties of religious or cultural significance to their Tribe. Federal agencies must make reasonable and good-faith efforts to identify and consult with relevant Tribes throughout the process.

Work With Experienced Section 106 Consultants

Section 106 compliance is a structured but nuanced process—and getting it right the first time saves time and money. From Phase I survey through MOA development and mitigation, TVAR's RPA-certified archaeologists have guided clients through hundreds of Section 106 reviews across the Southeast. We work closely with SHPOs, THPOs, and federal agencies to keep your project moving.

If you have an upcoming project with a federal nexus, reach out early. Early coordination is the single most effective way to control timelines and costs. Contact TVAR today to discuss your Section 106 requirements and how we can help.
excerpt: Learn what Section 106 of the NHPA requires, how the 4-step review process works, and what triggers compliance for federal agencies and private developers.
